entries.title: Viewing scan results and live tasks
entries.body:
# Where to view scan results and live tasks

When Burp runs a scan or a live task, its entire "inner world" (what was found, what was sent, what went wrong) is gathered in one place - on the **Dashboard** tab. Open the **Dashboard**, click the task you need in the **Tasks** panel, and the main part of the window expands into a set of tabs with details for that specific task.

This is an important point: the results tabs are tied to a SPECIFIC task. A project may have several scans and live tasks running at once, and each has its own set of tabs with its own numbers. Don't confuse this with the project-wide **All issues** / **Event log** lists at the bottom of the **Dashboard** - those aggregate across the whole project.

## What tabs there are

The set of tabs depends on the type of task and the phase it is in. The full list:

- **Summary** - an overview: task configuration, progress, top findings, recent events. Always present.
- **Issues** - vulnerabilities found. Appears only for tasks with an audit phase.
- **Audit items** - a list of individual HTTP requests that the task has taken into the audit (essentially, per-item progress of active checks). Only for tasks with an audit.
- **Event log** - a log of the task's key events: what started, what errors/warnings occurred. The main place to debug "why the scan is stuck".
- **Logger** - all HTTP traffic generated by this specific task (the scanner's requests and responses). Handy when you need to see exactly what Burp was sending.
- **Audit log** - a feed of events related to findings during the audit: an issue was found, evidence was added, a disappeared problem was removed. Only for tasks with an audit.
- **Live crawl view** - a render of the page the scanner is crawling right now (how it would look in a browser). Only for scans with an automatic crawl phase.

## How to use this in practice

```
Summary        -> quick status: what % is done, what it found, whether it's stuck
Issues         -> the findings themselves -> triage right away
Audit items    -> which requests the audit is running on and where it got stuck
Event log      -> debugging: connection errors, TLS, scope, timeouts
Logger         -> what exactly the scanner sent to the target (raw traffic)
Audit log      -> the chronology of issues appearing/being refined
Live crawl view-> visually see what the crawler is crawling right now
```

> The set of tabs changes on the fly. While the scan is only crawling (without an audit), the **Issues** / **Audit items** / **Audit log** tabs are not there yet - they appear once it reaches active checks. And vice versa: **Live crawl view** is visible only while the crawl is running.

> Practice: don't wait for the scan to finish. Even while it's working, open **Issues** and go through the findings by hand, and if something is going wrong (zero progress, nothing but errors) - dig into the **Event log**, the cause is usually visible right away there.

entries.title: Summary tab - task overview
entries.body:
# Summary - the whole task on one screen

The **Summary** tab is the "dashboard" of a specific task. It opens by default when you select a scan or live task in the **Tasks** panel on the **Dashboard**. You look here to understand in a couple of seconds: how the task is configured, how far it has progressed, and what it has already found. Available in both **Professional** and **Community**.

The screen is built from several blocks, and their composition depends slightly on the type of task (whether it has an audit, whether it has a crawl).

## Most serious vulnerabilities found

The top of the task's most serious findings - sorted by severity, then by confidence and name. This is a brief "digest" of the most important things, not the full list (the full one is on the **Issues** tab). The block is shown only if the task has an audit phase.

## Items added to site map

A summary of the new items the task discovered during the crawl and added to the site map (**Target > Site map**). This block appears for tasks that have a crawl but do NOT have an audit (that is, for a pure crawl scan) - which makes sense, since no vulnerabilities are looked for there, and what matters to show is precisely "what was found in the site's structure".

## Task configuration

An overview of how the task is configured: which scanning profile/configuration is selected, which phases are enabled, and so on. Handy for a quick sanity check that the scan was launched with the parameters you intended (and not, for example, with an aggressive audit where you wanted a light crawl).

## Task progress

The key execution metrics. Here you can see:

- **the number of requests made** - the overall volume of work;
- **the number of unique locations discovered** - how much the coverage has grown;
- **the number of errors** - the most important indicator of scan health.

This is the first thing you look at when diagnosing: if requests are going out but there are no locations or findings, or the error counter is climbing - something is wrong (scope, authentication, the target is blocking).

## Task log

A brief feed of the task's most recent actions - a chronology of what the scanner did in the recent past. Essentially a condensed version of the **Event log**, right inside the summary.

## Practice

```
Looked at Summary -> figured out: are requests going out? few errors? findings trickling in?
  all good      -> went off to triage in Issues
  pile of errors-> dropped into Event log to find the cause
  0 locations   -> checked scope/authentication/target availability
```

> Pitfall: a high **requests made** counter with zero findings and zero growth in locations is a classic sign that the scan is "banging against a wall": wrong scope, login required, or the target is throttling traffic. Don't wait for the end - figure it out right away.

entries.title: Audit items tab - per-item audit progress
Insertion points in audit items
entries.body:
# Insertion points - where the scanner injects payloads

An insertion point is a specific place in an HTTP request where the scanner inserts a payload during a check. Understanding which points Burp created for a request directly answers the question "what attack surface did it actually cover". The **Insertion points** panel opens inside the **Audit items** tab: you select an item and see all the insertion points found for its base request.

## Where the points come from: three categories

Burp doesn't limit itself to what's already in the request - it also tries to "expand" the surface:

- **Detected insertion points** - places based on data already present in the base request (existing parameters, headers, cookies, etc.).
- **Moved insertion points** - a parameter is relocated to a different part of the request (for example, a parameter from the URL into the message body) to test how it's handled there too. Only URL parameters, body parameters, and cookies can be moved.
- **Added insertion points** - entirely new parameters that Burp inserts on its own (for example, adding a body parameter to a POST request) to probe for hidden functionality.

## Nested insertion points

It happens that an application wraps data in several layers of encoding (a format within a format). These are **nested insertion points**: in the tree you can expand such a point and see the decoding functions and the decoded base value. This lets the scanner hit the "inner" data specifically, rather than its encoded wrapper.

## What is shown for each point

When you select a point, you see:

- **Name** - the parameter name;
- **Type** - the type (cookie, URL parameter, header, etc.); for nested ones the decoding function is also indicated;
- **Base value** - the original value from the base request.

## Point statuses

Each point has one of roughly thirteen statuses, showing what Burp did with it:

- **Pending / Auditing / Audited** - the normal flow: in the queue -> being checked -> checked;
- **Light-audited** - a lightened audit: frequently repeated points are checked in a trimmed way (for the sake of speed);
- **Skipped** - skipped. There are many reasons: configuration limits, a disabled insertion point type, a ban on relocating the parameter, the absence of a suitable scan check, and so on.

The panel can be collapsed - then the base response is shown instead.

## Practice

> When the scanner "found nothing" in a suspicious parameter - open its insertion point and check the status. **Skipped** or **Light-audited** explains the silence: the point was either not checked at all, or checked superficially. This is a signal to follow up by hand - send the request to **Repeater**/**Intruder** and poke the parameter yourself.

> Nested insertion points are a frequent source of missed bugs in APIs: the data sits in base64/URL-encoded JSON, and a superficial look doesn't see it. Burp can dig into them, but it's useful for you to know what's inside there too.

entries.title: Issues tab - vulnerabilities found by the task
Event log tab - the task event log
Audit log tab - the feed of events about findings
Live crawl view tab - what the crawler sees right now
entries.body:
# Live crawl view - a render of the page the scanner is crawling

The **Live crawl view** shows the page the scanner is crawling at this very moment, rendered the way it would look in a regular browser. This is a visual "window into the crawler's eyes": you can see what it's crawling right now and how the content it's parsing actually displays. The tab belongs to **Professional** and appears only for scans with an automatic crawl phase.

## Where to look

**Dashboard** -> select an active (or finished) scan in **Tasks** -> the **Live crawl view** tab. It works alongside the other results tabs (**Summary**, **Audit items**, **Issues**, **Event log**, **Audit log**).

## Why this is needed

- **watch the crawler in real time** - literally see which pages it's opening right now;
- **debug strange crawl behavior** - why it got stuck, where it isn't reaching, what it sees instead of the expected page;
- **tweak the configuration** based on what you see - if the crawler is clearly going the wrong way or running into a login/captcha, you notice it right away with your own eyes.

## Practice

> The crawl "isn't finding" half the application - open the **Live crawl view** and look at what actually renders. Frequent discoveries: instead of content there's a login page hanging (authentication is needed for the scan), or a captcha/block screen, or an empty SPA shell with no loaded data. A picture explains the problem faster than logs.

> This is an observation tool, not a control one - you can't click on the page and walk through it by hand here. For manually walking through the application, use **Burp's browser** and **Proxy**.

entries.title: Reporting on scan results - overview
entries.body:
# Reporting on scan results

Once the findings are sorted out, they need to be exported somewhere - to share with colleagues, a client, or to load into your own system. Burp can generate reports on the issues found. This is a **Professional** feature.

## Two formats to choose from

- **HTML** - the main "for humans" format: a ready report that's convenient to open in a browser, print, send to a client. It contains vulnerability descriptions, evidence, and remediation recommendations.
- **XML** - the "for machines" format: an export of issue data for importing into your own tools and reporting systems (ticket trackers, custom pipelines, etc.).

## How it's generated

Everything is done through the reporting wizard. The general logic:

1. You select the issues you want to get into the report (for example, in **Target > Site map** or on the **Issues** tab).
2. Through the context menu (right-click) you start the generation - an item along the lines of **Report selected issues** (or **Report issues for this host**, if you're reporting all findings for a host).
3. You go through the wizard's screens: format -> which details to include -> how to show requests/responses -> which issue types to include -> the report's own parameters -> where to save the file.

Detail on each screen and all the options is in a separate entry on report settings.

## Related capabilities

- PortSwigger has a sample ready HTML report - to see what it looks like;
- issues can be created manually, to include your own manual findings in the report (not only what the scanner found).

## Practice

> Before generating, run the findings through the filter and mark the noise: everything you didn't confirm by hand, set as **False positive** or delete. A report is only as good as the issue list is clean - the client doesn't need your unverified "candidates".

> For bug bounty, a full-fledged Burp report is usually overkill: programs need a short PoC with reproduction steps, not a 40-page HTML. An HTML report is more appropriate in a contracted pentest; XML - when the findings need to be machine-loaded somewhere else.

entries.title: Report settings - the export wizard
entries.body:
# Report settings - what to include in the export and how

Once you've selected issues and clicked **Report selected issues** (or **Report issues for this host**), the reporting wizard opens. It guides you through several screens; you move between them with the **Next** / **Back** buttons, and at the end you generate the file. Let's go through the screens in order and the key options of each.

## 1. Report format

A choice between two formats:

- **HTML** - tailored for printing and viewing in a browser.
- **XML** - for importing into third-party tools and frameworks.

For XML there's a separate important checkbox: **Base64-encode** the HTTP requests and responses inside the XML. This saves strict XML parsers from breaking on non-printable characters in the traffic - if you're going to parse the XML yourself, it's usually worth enabling.

## 2. Issue details

Which description blocks to pull into the report:

- **Issue background** - a general description of the vulnerability class;
- **Remediation background** - general remediation recommendations;
- **Issue detail** - details for the specific situation;
- **Remediation detail** - pinpoint recommendations for this case;
- **Vulnerability classifications** - links to classifications (for example, CWE).

## 3. HTTP messages

Separately for requests and for responses you choose how much traffic to include:

- **Do not include** - don't include at all;
- **Include relevant extract** - only the highlighted relevant fragments (in most cases this is enough);
- **Include in full** - the whole thing (with an optional length limit).

## 4. Selecting issue types

The wizard lists all the issue types from your selection with the count of each. Here you can uncheck minor/informational types so as not to bloat the report.

## 5. Report details (for HTML)

Configuring the look of the HTML report itself:

- **Report title** - the report title;
- the way to organize it (structure): group issues **by type**, **by severity**, or **by URL**;
- the table of contents depth (table of contents levels);
- a severity filter for summaries and charts (which severities to show in the summary/charts).

## 6. Saving the file

On the last step you click **Select file**, specify the path and name. For HTML the **.html** extension is mandatory. Then **Save** and **Next** - Burp generates the report.

## XML structure (for import)

If you parse the XML, the key elements are: `serialNumber`, `type`, `name`, `path`, `location`, `request`, `response` (the last two - with base64 attributes if you enabled encoding).

## Practice

```
A quick decent HTML report:
  Format = HTML
  Details = all blocks (background + detail + classifications)
  Messages = Include relevant extract (both req and resp)
  Issue types = uncheck Information noise
  Structure = by severity, a meaningful title
  Select file -> name.html -> Save -> Next
```

> **Include in full** for both requests AND responses on a large scan easily bloats the HTML to an unmanageable size and drags extra data into the report. By default **Include relevant extract** is enough - turn on the "full" mode selectively, when you really need the whole exchange.
